title: Gammon for Christmas – and for the rest of the year
excerpt: I know gammon on the Christmas table. My mother used to decorate it beautifully with cherries and pineapple rings. For me it remains one of the tastiest meats to eat. Gammon is lovely cold or warm. I'm putting this recipe up in good time for Christmas.

seo.title: Gammon for Christmas - and for the rest of the year - Marinda Kook

html: I know gammon on the Christmas table.  My mother used to decorate it beautifully with cherries and pineapple rings.  For me it remains one of the tastiest meats to eat.  Gammon is lovely cold or warm.  I'm putting this recipe up in good time for Christmas.

recipe.title: Gammon for Christmas – and for the rest of the year
recipe.author: Marinda Engelbrecht

image.alt: Gammon vir Krismis - en vir die res van die jaar

items:
10 ml mustard powder
125 ml apricot jam
125 ml brown sugar

steps: Roughly chop the onion and carrot into a pot.
Add the peppercorns, bay leaf and Ginger Ale to the carrots and onion.
Place your gammon in the pot and cook for 30 minutes for every 500g of meat (my 1,7kg cooked for 90 min).
Let the meat cool in the stock.
Now cut the thick skin loose from the fat with a sharp knife.
Cut the fat into diamonds.
Push a clove into each diamond.
Mix 125 ml brown sugar, 125 ml apricot jam and 10 ml mustard together.
Spread it generously over the meat.
Place the gammon back in the oven at 180 degrees celsius for around 30 minutes or until your gammon is nicely browned from the basting – you can even put it a little under the grill element.

notes: Enjoy this festive meat hot or cold with vegetables and salad. A little mustard on the side makes it even more delicious.
